title: The Radical, Unclassifiable Art of William Blake
description:
in Art, Books, Poetry |   	July 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

William Blake is a houseôÙhold name, or not far from it, but things get more co
body:
in Art, Books, Poetry |   	July 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

William Blake is a houseôÙhold name, or not far from it, but things get more comôÙpliôÙcatôÙed when it comes to proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal descripôÙtion. He was a poet, a painter, and a printôÙmakôÙer, at least insoôÙfar as he wrote poetôÙry, paintôÙed paintôÙings, and made prints. But we canãt hope to attain even a basic underôÙstandôÙing of his legaôÙcy if we regard him as one man who hapôÙpened to have the enerôÙgy to do three difôÙferôÙent things. In fact, the ostenôÙsiôÙbly sepôÙaôÙrate artisôÙtic purôÙsuits in which he engaged were but three aspects of a uniôÙfied act of creôÙation, resultôÙing in the likes of Songs of InnoôÙcence and of ExpeôÙriôÙenceô and his illuôÙmiôÙnatôÙed ãprophetôÙic booksã: unclasôÙsiôÙfiôÙable works by ãthe patron saint of unclasôÙsiôÙfiôÙable artists.ã
So Evan Puschak, betôÙter known as the NerdôÙwriter, labels him in his new video above. Blake made books, ãwhich he designed, wrote, etched, colôÙored, and printôÙed himôÙself, using a techôÙnique that he inventôÙed.ã They ãmix and synôÙtheôÙsize catôÙeôÙgories, and as a result, the artôÙwork of the late sevôÙenôÙteen- and earôÙly eighôÙteen-hunôÙdreds didôÙnãt realôÙly know what to make of them.ã
It didôÙnãt help that the RoyôÙal AcadôÙeôÙmy of Arts, foundôÙed when Blake was in adoôÙlesôÙcence, had laid down its own strict aesôÙthetôÙic, generôÙic, and forôÙmal stanôÙdards. OffiôÙcialôÙly an engraver and accordôÙed the lowôÙly staôÙtus thereôÙof, Blake devotôÙed his labors to realôÙizôÙing his elabôÙoôÙrateôÙly idioôÙsynôÙcratôÙic visions using images and words togethôÙer in ways that no artist had done before.
This choice ãto work as makôÙer of words, makôÙer of images, and crossôÙbreedôÙer of both, amountôÙed to a deciôÙsion to live in incomôÙmenôÙsuôÙrable neighôÙborôÙhoods of meanôÙing,ãô writesô The CamôÙbridge ComôÙpanôÙion to William Blake author MorôÙris Eaves as quotôÙed by Puschak. Blake swam against the curôÙrent ãof modôÙern human underôÙstandôÙing, whose bedrock is the prinôÙciôÙple of speôÙcialôÙizaôÙtion.ã Today, nearôÙly two cenôÙturies after his death, that prinôÙciôÙple still obtains, and in some ways more rigidôÙly than ever. But his work remains, from the Songsô and the prophetôÙic books to his illusôÙtraôÙtions of clasôÙsics like theô Divine ComôÙeôÙdy,ô ParôÙadise Lost, and even the BibôÙliôÙcal Book of Job, which he read as ãthe stoôÙry of a man who believes, misôÙtakôÙenôÙly, that salôÙvaôÙtion requires a slavôÙish obeôÙdiôÙence to words writôÙten in a book.ã With hisô rules, diviôÙsions, and catôÙeôÙgories, man has cutô realôÙiôÙty apart;ô through his art, Blake sought to make it whole again.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The RadôÙiôÙcal ArtisôÙtic & PhiloôÙsophôÙiôÙcal World of William Blake: A Short IntroôÙducôÙtion
William Blakeãs HalôÙluôÙciôÙnaôÙtoôÙry IllusôÙtraôÙtions of John Miltonãs ParôÙadise Lost
The OthôÙerôÙworldôÙly Art of William Blake: An IntroôÙducôÙtion to the VisionôÙary Poet and Painter
William Blake: The RemarkôÙable PrintôÙing Process of the EngôÙlish Poet, Artist & VisionôÙary
Enter an Archive of William Blakeãs FanôÙtasôÙtiôÙcal ãIlluôÙmiôÙnatôÙed Booksã: The Images Are SubôÙlime, and in High ResôÙoôÙluôÙtion
William Blakeãs 102 IllusôÙtraôÙtions of the Divine ComôÙeôÙdy ColôÙlectôÙed in a BeauôÙtiôÙful Book from Taschen
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: A Walk Through Homerãs Odyssey: A Guide to the Epic Before Seeing Christopher Nolanãs Film
description: Youãre gearôÙing up to see ChristoôÙpher Nolanãs Odyssey, but you havenãt read the HomeôÙrôÙic work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels lik
body:
Youãre gearôÙing up to see ChristoôÙpher Nolanãs Odyssey, but you havenãt read the HomeôÙrôÙic work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels like youãve nevôÙer read it at all. No worôÙries. Above, Tom HolôÙland and Dominic SandôÙbrook, the hosts of The Rest Is HisôÙtoôÙry podôÙcast, take you through the major plot lines of the Odyssey, unpackôÙing the litôÙerôÙary and hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal meanôÙing of the epicãs difôÙferôÙent tales. Itãs a good primerãjust what you need to get ready for one of the bigôÙger cinôÙeôÙma releasôÙes this year. Enjoy!
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtentô

title: 'Voices in the Mirror' Honors the Legacy of Pioneering Photographer Gordon Parks
description: From portraits taken around Harlem in the 1940s to assignments for Life magazine to the 1963 March on Washington, Gordon Parks (1912-2006) wielded
body:
From portraits taken around Harlem in the 1940s to assignments for Life magazine to the 1963 March on Washington, Gordon Parks (1912-2006) wielded his camera as a tool for social justice. He captured civil rights activists like Malcom X and Martin Luther King, Jr. in addition to artists and celebrities such as Helen Frankenthaler and Ingrid Bergman. But he may be best known for his candid portraits of families and communities in the segregated South during the era of Jim Crow. All of these and more will be on view in Voices in the Mirror at Jack Shainman Gallery in mid-September, also marking the 20th anniversary of The Gordon Parks Foundation.
Parks was spurred to pursue photography in 1937 after seeing photos taken for the Farm Security Administration (FSA), which held a mission to document American life. ãIô saw that the camera could be a weapon against poverty, against racism, against all sorts of social wrongs,ã he said. ãI knew at that point I had to have a camera.ã In 1942, he became the first Black photographer hired as part of the initiative, introducing him to Washington, D.C., where he noted that ãdiscrimination and bigotry were worse there than any place I had yet seen.ã
ãMartin Luther King, Jr., Washington, D.C.ã (1963), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches 
Among the images included in Voices in the Mirror are seminal portraits like ãAmerican Gothic, Washington, D.C.ã (1942), which captures a government worker named Ella Watson with a broom and mop. The work nods to American Regionalist painter Grant Woodãs likewise iconic ãAmerican Gothicã painting, created 12 years earlier as an ode to American values. Parksã image represented a starkly contrasted reality.
After speaking with Watson about her life and experience in D.C., Parks recalled that it was ãso disastrousô that I felt that I must photograph this woman in a way that would make me feelãor make the public feelãabout what Washington, D.C., was in 1942.ã He positioned her in front of a flag with a symbolic mop and broom. ãI didnãt care about what anybodyô else felt,ã he said. ãThatãs what I felt about America and Ella Watsonãs position inside America.ã
The exhibition is accompanied by numerous anecdotes and reflections by some of those who appeared in the photos or had close relationships with those who did, such as Malcolm Xãs daughter, Qubilah Shabazz, and Cora Taylor, who was one of the women standing near a pair of segregated water fountains in ãSegregation in the Southã (1956).
Voices in the Mirror opens on September 18 and continues through November 7 in New York. You might also be interested in the works of other FSA photographers who documented the South during the 1930s and 1940s, such as Russell Lee and Marion Post Wolcott.
ãAmerican Gothic, Washington D.C.ã (1942), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches
ãAt Segregated Drinking Fountain, Mobile, Alabamaã (1956), archival pigment print, 34 x 34 inches
ãUntitled, Shady Grove, Alabamaã (1956), archival pigment print, 48 x 48 inches
ãUntitled, Harlem, New Yorkã (1963), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches 
ãUntitled, Alabamaã (1956), archival pigment print, 42 x 42 inches
ãWoman and Dog in Window, Harlem, New Yorkã (1943), gelatin silver print, 34 x 34 inches 
ãMr. and Mrs. Albert Thornton, Mobile, Alabama, 1956 (1956), archival pigment print, 50 x 50 inches
ãStore Front, Mobile, Alabamaã (1956), archival pigment print, 48 x 48 inches

title: A View From the Easel
description: Welcome to the 346th installment of A View From the Easel, a series in which artists reflect on their workspace. This week, Patrick Guett
body:
Welcome to the 346th installment of A View From the Easel, a series in which artists reflect on their workspace. This week, Patrick Guetta repurposes brand imagery to spread environmentalist messages and approaches the streets of Los Angeles as mini museums in themselves.Want to take part? Check out ourô submission guidelinesô and share a bit about your studio with us throughô this form! All mediums and workspaces are welcome, including your home studio.Patrick Guetta, Los Angeles, CaliforniaHow long have you been working in this space?Iãve been working in this space for two years. First, I had to build the studio itself and give it energy. Little by little, it transformed completely from an empty space into a place filled with ideas, colors, creation, and dreams.Describe an average day in your studio.I wake up slowly and try not to rush unless I have a meeting. Usually I start around 10 or 11, and I let the day unfold organically. I try not to put boundaries on creativity and let inspiration take over. Sometimes I work late, sometimes I leave and come back, and sometimes answering one email suddenly turns into creating. Itãs a very organic process.How does the space affect your work?The space is very important because it feels like home, so Iãm very comfortable in it. Itãs sunny, filled with natural light, very private, and peaceful. That atmosphere helps me disconnect from the outside world and immerse myself in the creative process. Sometimes I can completely lose myself in the work for hours.How do you interact with the environment outside your studio?I can walk practically everywhere and rarely use my car. Iãm surrounded by Fairfax, Melrose, and Beverly areas filled with graffiti, street art, energy, and creativity. I walk constantly through the neighborhood, to coffee shops, and around the city. Itãs a very inspiring place to be in Los Angeles, and I feel lucky to have my studio in the middle of all that energy.What do you love about your studio?What I love most about my studio is that itãs mine. I designed it around the way I like to work and live creatively. Itãs very private, peaceful, and allows me to fully focus without distractions. It feels personal, comfortable, and completely connected to my energy.What do you wish were different?Honestly, I donãt wish anything were different. I feel very lucky. My studio is an extension of my home, and my home is an extension of my studio. Everything feels connected and comfortable for the way I live and create. Iãm not looking to grow into something bigger right now. I just want the freedom to create peacefully and stay inspired.What is your favorite local museum?I donãt really have one favorite museum because each one has a different creative energy. But honestly, one of my favorite museums is the street itself. I love walking through Los Angeles, looking at billboards, signs, graffiti, and visual culture. My work comes from observing what people create, say, and consume. In a way, I turn those everyday surroundings into art through the way I see them.What is your favorite art material to work with?I really like working with acrylic paint because of the vivacity and intensity of the colors. It translates the energy of my work very naturally. I also love photography and use it a lot in my creative process. Sculpture interests me too, but itãs a much more complicated process. Acrylic on canvas feels immediate, spontaneous, and connected to the way I like to create.
                  
                      
                        Community
                      
                      
                        A View From the Easel

title: Man Sentenced to 20 Years for Fatally Stabbing NYC Vogue Dancer
description:
News
                Dmitriy Popov killed the Black gay dancer O'Shae Sibley at a Brooklyn gas station in 2023 in a homophobic h
body:
News
                Dmitriy Popov killed the Black gay dancer O'Shae Sibley at a Brooklyn gas station in 2023 in a homophobic hate crime.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            A photo of late professional dancer O'Shae Sibley attending a class at the Ailey Extension's drop in program (photo Whitney Browne for Ailey Extension, courtesy Ailey Extension)Nearly three years since the attack, Dmitriy Popov was handed a 20-year sentence today, July 16, over the fatal stabbing of Black gay professional dancer O'Shae Sibley at a gas station in Midwood, Brooklyn. The sentencing comes after Popov was convicted of manslaughter as a hate crime in June, as the 2023 stabbing happened after he and a group of friends confronted Sibley's group with homophobic slurs because the dancer was vogueing to music at a gas pump.ãOãShae Sibley was simply being himself ã a Black gay man enjoying a summer evening ã but his mere existence caused this defendant to curse, harass and ultimately kill him,ã said Brooklyn District Attorney Eric Gonzalez upon announcing Popov's sentence.Hailing from Philadelphia, 28-year-old Sibley was a performer with the Philadanco professional dance troupe. After moving to Brownsville, Brooklyn in 2020, he became a devoted student at the Alvin Ailey Dance Theater's drop-in classes. Sibley integrated his love of movement with his identity as a proud Black gay man, and in his advocacy for social change and racial justice through public performances and projects directed by his close friend, choreographer Kemar Jewel. After a day at the beach on July 29, 2023, Sibley and his friends stopped to fill gas at a station on Coney Island Avenue after 11 pm. Still clad in swimming trunks, the group began dancing to a Beyoncûˋ song playing from the car, and Popov's band of friends began taunting Sibley's group with homophobic and racial slurs. The two groups were in a heated argument for a few minutes before ultimately dispersing, but Popov, who was 17 at the time, took out his phone and began filming while reportedly continuing to disparage Sibley and his friends. Sibley turned around and confronted Popov while a witness tried to intervene. He chased Popov to the sidewalk area, where the teenager ultimately pulled out a five-and-a-half-inch blade and stabbed Sibley in the torso. The dancer was pronounced dead after arriving at Maimonides Hospital, and Popov was on the lam for about a week before turning himself in. Popov initially pled not guilty and testified that he acted in self-defense and not hate after Sibley approached again, but failed to convince the jury that convicted him last month.ãI hope today's sentence will bring some closure to OãShaeãs many loved ones and to the entire LGTBQ+ community, which has been shaken by this tragic and deeply upsetting crime,ã Gonzalez said. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New York
                      
                      
                        Brooklyn

title: Planned Charlie Kirk Statue Gets Trolled
description:
Daily Newsletter
                Also: Trump shrinks two national monuments in Utah by 90%.
body:
Daily Newsletter
                Also: Trump shrinks two national monuments in Utah by 90%.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Today in the news, an artist announces his plan to install a monument to Charlie Kirk in Times Square this fall. Let's just say the renderings don't bode well. Meanwhile, the Trump administration cuts the size of two national monuments in Utah ã rich with rock art and sacred land ã by a staggering 90% while also pushing for "energy development" in the area.ô ô ô ô Also: A docudrama attempts to shed new light on Caravaggio's life, Required Reading, Art Movements, and lots more. ãHakim Bishara, editor-in-chief


	
		
			Artistãs Planned Monument to Charlie Kirk Gets Trolled Online
		
	
	
		New York City sculptor Sergio Furnari is being raked over the coals online over his plan to unveil a memorial sculpture of slain right-wing podcaster Charlie Kirk in Times Square.

Thousands have flocked to Furnariãs Instagram comments to troll and threaten the forthcoming memorial, which will reportedly be unveiled on September 10 in recognition of the first anniversary of Kirkãs assassination.
	
	
		Read More
	


                    
                    
                    
                        
                            The ICA at VCU Brings Immersive Installation ãDeo Vindiceã to RichmondThe gallery-sized installation at the Institute for Contemporary Art combines Greek mythology with Civil War history to examine white supremacy and hegemonic power.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsThe Trump administration hasô significantly diminished the size of two national monuments in Utah that hold sacred lands and richô archaeological sites.The Manhattan DA said three antiquities are being repatriated to Mexico. One of them was previously held by the Metropolitan Museum of Art.From Our Critics


	
		
			Required Reading
		
	
	
		This week: Chitra Ganeshãs futuristic myths, Andrûˋ Breton and optimism, the mermaids of Florida, a Palestinian digital archive, Argentina and racism, and more.
	
	
		Read More
	

Brilliantly insightful and curious article. Fascinating how the real, superreal, and unreal combine in corporate story telling to the opposite end of actual community, culture, or worthwhile tradition.Randy Polumbo on ãHow a Basket Empire Wove the Myth of AmericaãFrom the Archive

title: Trevor Winkfieldãs Cheerfully Sinister Worlds
description:
Art Review
                His rejection of certainty is an urgent reminder that imagination should not be neglected in the purs
body:
Art Review
                His rejection of certainty is an urgent reminder that imagination should not be neglected in the pursuit of art. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Trevor Winkfield, "Voyage II" (1996), acrylic on linen (all photos courtesy Tibor de Nagy gallery)The English-born Trevor Winkfield has taken a unique path that has yet to be fully recognized for its unlikeliness, depth, and breadth. After attending art school in Leeds and getting his masterãs degree from the Royal College of Art in 1967, he moved to New York in 1969, where he continued to publish a poetry magazine, Juillard (1968ã72), and made a name for himself among a group of writers and artists known as the New York School. He worked for Arthur Cohen, who sold books and ephemera on 20th-century Modern Art movements such as Dada and Surrealism and made entries to Cohenãs encyclopedic catalogs. These were informative, succinct, and witty. He was a polymath whose knowledge of minor artists and writers, such as Gerald Murphy and Ronald Firbank, was unmatched.In 1976, Winkfield began painting again, made book covers, and collaborated with poets such as John Ashbery, Charles North, Ron Padgett, and Harry Matthews. He had his New York debut exhibition in 1977, and published his translation of Raymond Rousselãs How I Wrote Certain of My Books. Roussel, who is widely considered the most eccentric writer of the 20th century, wrote according to linguistic rules based on homophones, words that sound similar but have different meanings. As one might expect, in the years since, critics have tried to connect Winkfieldãs collage-like paintings of artificial worlds with Rousselãs wildly improbable novels, but have never discovered the key, which I feel was never there. As literary as Winkfield is, he is equally a visual person. This complete duality is what makes him inimitable.Trevor Winkfield, "The Astrologer" (2001), acrylic on linenWinkfield paints on a table; the eight works in The Mermaidãs Revenge - Paintings 1991-2001 at Tibor de Nagy gallery come from a decade when Winkfield had access to an apartment where he was able to work on what were for him large-scale surfaces. Done in acrylic on linen, they measure between 35 1/2 x 19 1/2 inches (~90.1ô x 49.5 cm) and 45 x 72 inches (~1.1 x 1.8 m). The size of none of the works is the same.ô All of them are packed with details meticulously done in flat, saturated, playroom colors, which are interrupted by a volumetric detail done in a straightforward, illustrative style. One reason for this disruption is that Winkfield doesnãt want us to read the paintings as airless and flat. He has conjured an artificial world ã simultaneously brightly colored, slightly sinister, and cheerfully incomprehensible ãô usually inhabited by a delicately featured young man engaged in a mysterious activity.The paintingãs titles underscore the solitary nature of the tasks being undertaken: ãThe Navigatorã (2000), ãThe Studentã (1999), ãThe Astrologerã (1991), and ãSelf-Portraitã (2001), with its palette and carefully depicted daubs of paint. Each figure could be a surrogate for Winkfield, an artist and writer who has written smart and original pieces on Georges Braque, Jasper Johns, John Graham, and Florine Stettheimer, collected in Georges Braque and Others: The Selected Art Writings of Trevor Winkfield, 1990ã2009 (2014).Trevor Winkfield, "The Student" (1999), acrylic on linenIn ãThe Student,ã a young man wearing a helmet that includes a fish and what might be a lock for an eyepiece is holding a long-handled pot beneath a lightbulb that is releasing tokens marked by a snowflake pattern. The tokens reappear in the sky above the pink and pale-blue mountain seen in the open window. The studentãs body and clothes are made up of separate parts, with his oversized hands and bare feet painted different colors, including celadon and orange. The flatness of the shapes suggests we are looking at an intricate shadow puppet engaged in an activity whose meaning we will never unravel.ô The hermetic nature of Winkfieldãs paintings might be off-putting to some, but not to me. I think his devotion to creating a completely artificial world is a necessary and important counterpoint to the art worldãs belief in literal meaning and transparent, socially conscious subject matter. His rejection of certainty and the comfort it supposedly provides is an urgent reminder that imagination should not be neglected in the pursuit of art, and of the freedom it can give you. In the unlikely synthesis of bright colors, incomprehensible behavior, and compelling scenarios, we are invited to enter a meticulously constructed world unlike any other.Left: Trevor Winkfield, "The Navigator" (2000), acrylic on linen; right: Trevor Winkfield, "Self Portrait" (2001), acrylic on linenTrevor Winkfield: The Mermaid's Revenge - Paintings 1991 - 2001 continues at Tibor de Nagy gallery (11 Rivington Street, Lower East Side, Manhattan) through July 31. The exhibition was organized by the gallery. 
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Trevor Winkfield
                      
                      
                        Tibor de Nagy
                      
                      
                        New York
